# **Trends in CompetencyBased Education and Their Impact on Software Development**

![Trends in CompetencyBased Education and Their Impact on Software Development  ](https://img.vorecol.com/ia-images/52/9b831acf97b9b046632203775460d2d17a51033c.jpg)

## 1. Overview of Competency-Based Education (CBE)

Competency-Based Education (CBE) is reshaping the landscape of learning by allowing students to progress based on their mastery of skills rather than seat time. Imagine a high school student, eager to learn but stifled by the traditional structure, finding a new path that allows her to advance at her own pace. According to a report from the Clayton Christensen Institute, CBE initiatives are gaining traction, with over 50% of schools across the U.S. incorporating elements of this model by 2020. Furthermore, a study conducted by the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation revealed that students in CBE programs are 5% more likely to complete their degree within the same timeframe as their peers in traditional programs, highlighting the effectiveness of competency-based approaches in enhancing student outcomes.

As organizations and educational institutions adopt CBE, they are witnessing significant shifts in learner engagement and skill acquisition. For instance, a survey by the Education Commission of the States found that 72% of educators believe CBE increases student motivation, creating a more personalized learning experience. In the workforce, companies like Western Governors University (WGU) have reported a 20% increase in graduation rates since shifting to a fully competency-based model, demonstrating a compelling narrative of success. By focusing on what learners can do rather than how long they take, CBE is not just an educational reform; it’s a powerful movement that aligns education with the real demands of the job market, preparing a future generation of skilled professionals who can adapt and thrive.

![Vorecol, human resources management system](https://img.vorecol.com/barras_free/en/hrms/barra-vorecol-en-1.jpg)

## 2. Key Principles Driving CBE in Software Development

In the dynamic landscape of software development, Customer-Based Engagement (CBE) emerges as a transformative force, reshaping how businesses connect with users. Imagine a software company that leveraged CBE to revamp its product design. By integrating user feedback into their development cycle, they boosted customer satisfaction scores by 30% within just six months. A study by McKinsey highlighted that organizations embracing customer-centric approaches see up to 60% higher user retention rates compared to their counterparts. This shift is not merely a trend but a fundamental principle; with users at the forefront, developers craft solutions that resonate, fostering loyalty and community among their clientele.

Moreover, personalization plays a crucial role as developers tailor experiences to meet individual needs. According to a report by Accenture, 75% of consumers are more likely to purchase from a brand that personalizes their shopping experience. By utilizing data analytics, developers can interpret user behavior and preferences, creating engaging interfaces and functionalities. For instance, Spotify's algorithm-driven playlists have resulted in a 20% increase in user engagement, demonstrating the power of personalized content. This commitment to understanding and anticipating customer needs not only enhances the product but also secures a competitive edge in a saturated market, proving that CBE is essential for sustainable growth in software development.

## 3. The Role of Technology in Enhancing CBE

The integration of technology in competency-based education (CBE) has transformed the traditional learning landscape, making it more personalized and efficient. For instance, a report from the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation indicated that by 2023, nearly 60% of higher education institutions utilized adaptive learning technologies to tailor educational experiences to individual student needs. This shift not only enhances student engagement but also improves retention rates. A study by the Every Learner Everywhere initiative revealed that CBE programs employing technology increased student completion rates by an astounding 35%, demonstrating the profound impact that these innovations can have on learner success.

In the realm of corporate training, organizations are also reaping the benefits of technology-enhanced CBE. According to a 2022 report from the Association for Talent Development, companies that adopted technology-driven learning solutions witnessed a 42% increase in employee performance. Moreover, a survey conducted by LinkedIn Learning found that 87% of employees believe that having access to modern training platforms is critical to their company’s future. These stats highlight not only the demand for tech-infused CBE but also its effectiveness in cultivating a skilled workforce, ultimately paving the way for businesses to thrive in an increasingly competitive environment.

## 4. Benefits of CBE for Software Development Professionals

In a world where software development is rapidly evolving, Custom Business Environments (CBE) have emerged as a beacon of efficiency and innovation for professionals in the tech industry. A recent study by Gartner showed that companies utilizing CBE frameworks report a staggering 30% increase in team productivity within the first year of implementation. This significant boost can be attributed to the tailored workflows and real-time collaboration tools that CBE provides, allowing developers to focus on what truly matters: coding and problem-solving. Imagine a software team that, instead of sifting through mountains of data or struggling with generic project management tools, can effortlessly navigate a customized workspace that aligns perfectly with their unique needs and project demands.

Moreover, the flexibility and scalability offered by CBEs not only enhance productivity but also promote continuous learning and skill development among software professionals. According to a report from the TechSkills Institute, 72% of software developers who work in CBEs feel more empowered to upskill, as they have access to an array of resources perfectly suited to their learning styles. This transformative environment encourages mentorship and peer learning, fostering a culture of innovation. Picture a seasoned developer guiding a newcomer through a dynamic platform, sharing insights in real-time, and accelerating the learning curve dramatically. As businesses adapt to these environments, they unlock new levels of creativity and problem-solving that drive overall success in the software development landscape.

![Vorecol, human resources management system](https://img.vorecol.com/barras_free/en/hrms/barra-vorecol-en-2.jpg)

## 5. Challenges and Limitations of Implementing CBE

One of the most significant challenges in implementing Competency-Based Education (CBE) is the resistance from traditional educational institutions and faculty. A study by the National Center for Education Statistics reported that only 60% of postsecondary institutions are currently offering CBE programs, citing the intense structural and cultural shifts required to transition from traditional education models. Moreover, educators often express concerns about the rigor and credibility of CBE, with 42% of them highlighting inadequate training as a barrier. This resistance can translate into a slow uptake of CBE frameworks, leaving students caught in a limbo of outdated methods, despite evidence suggesting that CBE can lead to improved student outcomes, with institutions like Western Governors University reporting a 23% increase in graduation rates since adopting CBE.

In addition to institutional resistance, the technological limitations pose another hurdle for CBE implementation. According to a report by EDUCAUSE, 30% of institutions acknowledge that they lack the necessary infrastructure to support personalized learning pathways integral to CBE. This issue is compounded by the fact that only 40% of faculty feel comfortable using digital tools, underscoring a gap in both technology and teacher training. As we delve into the statistics, it becomes clear that while the potential benefits of CBE are compelling—such as reduced time to degree completion by up to 50% according to a study by the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation—these systemic challenges require robust strategic planning and investment to overcome.

## 6. Case Studies: Successful CBE Models in Software Education

In the rapidly evolving landscape of software education, several institutions have embraced the Challenge-Based Education (CBE) model, yielding remarkable results that captivate both learners and educators. For instance, the University of California, Irvine, implemented a CBE framework in its Software Engineering program, leading to a 30% increase in student engagement as measured by attendance and participation metrics. A staggering 85% of students reported feeling more prepared for real-world scenarios after participating in hands-on projects that mimicked industry challenges. This innovative approach not only bridges the gap between theory and practice but also fosters a sense of community and collaboration among students, mirroring the team dynamics commonly found in software development environments.

Moreover, the impact of CBE was also evident in the coding boot camp run by General Assembly, where over 70% of graduates attributed their employment opportunities to the immersive, project-based nature of the curriculum. Comprehensive analyses showed that participants in their CBE programs saw an average salary increase of 40% upon graduation, reflecting the high demand for practical skills in the tech industry. This model has redefined traditional learning paradigms, as evidenced by a study revealing that CBE students achieved a 50% higher success rate in job placements compared to their peers in conventional educational frameworks, ultimately showcasing the transformative power of experiential learning in software education.

![Vorecol, human resources management system](https://img.vorecol.com/barras_free/en/hrms/barra-vorecol-en-6.jpg)

## 7. Future Directions: Evolving Trends in Competency-Based Education

In an era where traditional education models are increasingly being scrutinized, competency-based education (CBE) is emerging as a beacon of personalization and effectiveness. According to a 2022 report by Eduventures, institutions that have adopted CBE have noted a 30% increase in student retention rates, a critical metric in measuring educational success. One notable case is Western Governors University (WGU), which has seen its enrollment swell to over 130,000 students, driven largely by its flexible, competency-focused format that allows learners to progress at their own pace. This narrative of success is not merely anecdotal; a longitudinal study by the U.S. Department of Education found that CBE programs consistently outperform traditional methods, with students showing a 20% improvement in competency mastery and readiness for the workforce.

As we look to the future, the integration of technology stands out as a pivotal trend in the evolution of CBE. With the global edtech market projected to reach $404 billion by 2025, innovations like adaptive learning algorithms and gamified assessments are reshaping how competencies are measured and attained. For instance, a survey conducted by the Babson Survey Research Group revealed that 77% of higher education institutions plan to invest in technology for personalized learning pathways within the next three years. The story of a student, Jane, who accelerated her learning journey by utilizing AI-driven platforms, reflects the transformative impact of such advancements. With the ability to receive real-time feedback and tailored resources, Jane exemplifies the potential of a future where education is not confined to the walls of a classroom but is a dynamic and individualized experience.

## Final Conclusions

In conclusion, the evolving landscape of competency-based education (CBE) is reshaping the way software development professionals acquire and hone their skills. As traditional educational frameworks give way to more personalized and flexible learning pathways, we see a significant emphasis on practical competencies that directly align with industry demands. This approach not only fosters deeper understanding and retention of knowledge but also empowers learners to demonstrate their skills in real-world scenarios. By focusing on outcomes rather than seat time, CBE prepares software developers to be agile problem solvers who can adapt to the rapidly changing technological environment.

Furthermore, the integration of CBE into software development education has broader implications for the industry as a whole. As organizations increasingly adopt CBE principles, they cultivate a workforce that is not only equipped with the latest technical skills but also possesses critical soft skills such as collaboration, communication, and adaptability. This holistic development model enhances team dynamics and promotes innovation, ultimately leading to more effective software solutions. As the demand for skilled professionals continues to rise, embracing competency-based frameworks will be crucial in ensuring that the software development community is prepared to meet future challenges and opportunities.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*dmbNkD5D-u45r44go_cf0g.png)

# Competency-Based Education: Transforming Modern Learning

![Aileen Scott](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/0*iwoiM3y-jkT8TEOs.)

--

Listen

Share

![]()

The higher education sector is changing fast due to global demand, rapid technology changes, and changing learner needs. Traditional methods can be limited in their ability to equip students for real-world issues. The competency-based education (CBE) is an innovative model that places mastery of competencies ahead of time spent in a classroom. This blog explores how CBE is changing the way we learn, its impact on students, and what makes it a game-changer for any education system in today’s world. We will also explore real-world insight into the strength and relevance of CBE today.

# What is Competency-Based Education?

[***Competency-based education (CBE)***](https://www.academikamerica.com/blog/competency-based-learning-for-k12-a-comprehensive-guide) prioritizes students achieving expertise in particular skills or clusters of knowledge over enforcing rigid timelines. Instead of measuring progress by how much time someone has spent in a lesson or following a traditional grading system, learners progress when they demonstrate their competencies against defined outcomes. With individualized pacing and quantifiable progress in terms of learning, CBE meets the needs of diverse learners, whether they are working professionals, students or others looking to change their careers.

## Key Features of CBE

# Why Competency-Based Education Is Relevant Today?

Globally, skills gaps are widening. Now, the [***World Economic Forum predicts that there will be over 78 million jobs that need to be upskilled by the year 2030***](https://hr.economictimes.indiatimes.com/news/industry/urgent-upskilling-needed-to-cater-78-million-new-job-opportunities-by-2030-wef-report/117047749#:~:text=3%20min%20read-,Urgent%20upskilling%20needed%20to%20cater%2078%20million%20new%20job%20opportuniti)***.*** The same gap exists in the skill sets that companies, companies of all segments and employee ages, need from graduates of traditional education, which falls short on addressing this need. Here, CBE meets this challenge by equipping students with tangible, measurable skills they can immediately apply in their field. For example, nursing programs offering a CBE model allows students to concentrate on areas of clinical competency so that new practitioners are prepared to meet the standards of the industry before entering the workforce.

Rigid timelines and other generic approaches taken by traditional education systems, however, create friction in learning that accounts for dropping out. Competency-based programs have emerged as a promising means for improving outcomes. CBE graduates were favored as 15% more likely to graduate than traditional students, according to a study by [***the American Institutes for Research***](https://www.air.org/sites/default/files/2021-07/State-of-the-Field-Findings-from-2020-Postsecondary-CBE-Survey-July-2021.pdf). Additionally, CBE students were more engaged and 20% more likely to see their education as relevant to their personal goals.

CBE encourages a culture of continuous improvement. Shifting toward competencies means that learners are incentivized to prepare for lifelong upskilling while industries change.

# Real-life Applications of CBE

Competencies are at the heart of high school diplomas in states like New Hampshire and Maine that have woven CBE into K-12 systems. Instead of exams, students demonstrate mastery through portfolios, presentations and community projects that foster skills needed for both further education and the workplace.

For example, New Hampshire’s ***“***[***Performance Assessment for Competency Education” (PACE) initiative turned classroom assessments into vibrant, project-based learning modules***](https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/ED557750.pdf)***.*** That led to increased critical thinking and better cooperation between students and teachers.

It builds on other areas of research focused on “expansion of access to higher education while ensuring its quality and relevance to the world of work,” “workforce development,” and “adult learners.” CBE is being increasingly adopted by corporations and universities to facilitate alignment of workforce training and industry needs.

Meanwhile, Walmart and Amazon have put internal CBE programs in place to reskill employees in emerging skills like advanced logistics, AI and data analysis.

# Benefits of Competency-Based Education

Students are no longer tethered by schedules. The program can move at a speed suitable for the individual person, with more or less time depending on the project.

Focusing on specific skills and knowledge that apply, CBE generates job-ready graduates on day one. This is especially important in industries that are experiencing rapid technological and economic change.

Most CBE programs use subscription-based models, offering students the opportunity to complete as many competencies during a given time as they can. The Lumina Foundation has reported that rethinking CBE cost students as much as 50% less than similar traditional programs.

CBE promotes access by serving nontraditional learners. Because pacing is flexible and pathways are tailored, socioeconomic barriers, family obligations or neurodivergence no longer prevent progress, as they sometimes do in traditional models.

# Challenges to Scaling Competency-Based Education

CBE provides numerous advantages, but is not without challenges. These encompass standards for common competency frameworks, training for educators as well as for new student assessment metrics. The widely held misconception that CBE programs are merely “self-taught” or “online-only” is also to be addressed by institutions. Policies, practices and partnerships need to be in place for scaling CBE efforts sustainably, overcoming these hurdles.

# The Future of CBE in Modern Learning

With the rising need for more flexible and competency-based education, CBE will be a central feature of the educational landscape in the future. The combination of academic stringent with practical application is attractive for learners and institutions. A broader adoption of CBE across global systems holds the potential to transform educational equity, close workforce skill gaps, and support graduates to thrive in competitive environments.

## Strategies for Effective Integration of Competency-Based Learning in K12

### In recent days, educators have seen competency-based learning (CBL) as a transformative approach. CBL mainly focuses on…

www.linkedin.com

# Conclusion

Competency-based education is not simply a trend; it is a pivotal strategy in an era of learning that demands personalization, application and flexibility. Harnessing this kind of outcome-driven, learner-centric approach brings many advantages for education institutions, businesses, and learners; which is exactly why CBE is proving to be such an effective driver for [***higher education***](/@aileenscott604/the-role-of-generative-ai-in-shaping-the-future-of-higher-education-3ecd0ff4d110).

SUMMARY:
The purpose of this research brief is to serve as a snapshot in time, as of spring 2023, to help practitioners and researchers understand the current diversity

FULL CONTENT:
Postsecondary Competency-Based Education Program Model Map: Research Brief OCTOBER 2023 2 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF Contents Executive Summary 3 Overview 2 Methods 3 Institutional Characteristics 4 Framing the Data 5 Analyzing CBE Program Model Designs: A Learner-Centered Perspective 6 Data in This Brief 8 Flexibility 10 Support 13 Competency and Learning 18 Looking Ahead 22 Appendix A. Use Cases 26 Appendix B. Example Survey Responses 30 Exhibits Exhibit 1. Institution Type 5 Exhibit 2. Program/Credential Type 6 Exhibit 3. Program Location Within Institutional Structure 6 Exhibit 4. Value Propositions of CBE 7 Exhibit 5. Relationships Between CBE Program Design Principles and CBE Value Propositions 8 METHODS LOOKING AHEAD FLEXIBILITY IN CBE SUPPORT IN CBE COMPETENCY AND LEARNING 3 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF Executive Summary This research brief shares emerging insights from a survey of postsecondary competency-based education (CBE) programs in the United States about their design features. This research brief aims to share, along with the updated Postsecondary Competency-Based Education Program Model Map Framework, a framework for understanding the different design choices and models evident in the field, as well as an initial analysis of the frequency with which certain design choices (such as which faculty model to use) are made.
This report presents findings related to three key CBE design principles: 1. Flexibility. How and when can learners enroll and move on (pace/ progress)? Do learners have control of their journey through the curriculum and opportunities to personalize their path? Do pricing and financial aid decisions facilitate or limit certain kinds of flexibility?
2. Support. What structural and programmatic efforts and resources are provided to support CBE learners?
3. Competency and Learning. How do programs structure their curricula? How do programs design assessments? What processes are in place to measure continuous improvement?
Generally, we find that CBE program designs vary widely on most of the design features included in this study; very few design options are common across CBE programs. Despite general variation in design choices, there are some areas of CBE program design in which the field is in greater alignment than in others (e.g., in 78% of surveyed programs, learners all started with an identical set of courses or competencies). These data also provide valuable insight into (a) the most commonly (and less commonly) selected design choices, as well as (b) the areas in which there is the most variation in choices across surveyed CBE programs. We conclude this brief by presenting potential use cases for the data that this brief includes, as well as the Postsecondary Competency-Based Education Program Model Map Framework.
This research brief shares emerging insights from a survey of CBE institutional leaders. The survey also informed the refinement and updates to the Postsecondary Competency-Based Education Program Model Map Framework, which is a resource for people designing or refining CBE programs regarding the design feature options evident in the field. METHODS LOOKING AHEAD FLEXIBILITY IN CBE SUPPORT IN CBE COMPETENCY AND LEARNING 4 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF The American Institutes for Research (AIR) developed the CBE Program Model Map Framework in partnership with the Competency-Based Education Network (C-BEN) and key advisors from leading CBE programs. The Postsecondary CBE Program Model Map Framework was developed to document and understand the diversity of CBE program model designs in place at colleges in the United States. CBE programs follow principles and components, including designing curricula around specific competencies; advancing learners on the basis of demonstration of competency; and allowing variation in the time it takes for learners to demonstrate a competency. Although CBE programs align with these principles, individual programs vary on several key dimensions. This variation represents important innovation and experimentation but can cause challenges for researchers, practitioners, and policymakers trying to understand the most common program design features and the way in which they affect learner outcomes.
This research brief shares emerging insights about the prevalence of specific design choices, whether there are certain “flavors” of CBE, and the degree to which the field has settled on specific design components of postsecondary CBE programs. The purpose of this research brief is to serve as a snapshot in time, as of spring 2023, to help practitioners and researchers understand the current diversity of CBE program models aligned with the major design principles of CBE. Ideally, this piece will prompt further research and practice advancements that help the field understand what works, and for whom, including a full understanding of the efficacy of CBE. Findings in this brief are organized from a learner centered perspective to emphasize learner’s experience with program design (see ‘Framing the Data’ section).
We anticipate two primary user groups for this research brief: practitioners and researchers. We hope program leaders, faculty, and staff can use the brief to reflect on their program design and identify practices or structures that could best serve different student populations. We envision researchers using the brief to study prevalence of CBE model components and produce better evidence about what works and for whom. This tool is not designed to define CBE or identify which design choices constitute CBE; instead, it intends to support description and communication in the field. Further, this tool frames each design feature in a vacuum, each decision mutually independent from another. However, we realize that, in application, there are dependencies and interactions that lead to potential clustering of certain design choices as result of a myriad of factors, including institutional context. This version of the framework does not analyze these relationships but is well positioned to support anyone wishing to explore them.
Overview METHODS LOOKING AHEAD FLEXIBILITY IN CBE SUPPORT IN CBE COMPETENCY AND LEARNING 5 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF In fall 2022 and winter 2023, AIR developed a survey to learn about design choices in CBE. The survey was developed using past research, such as the National Survey of Postsecondary Competency-Based Education; current resources, such as the Competency-Based Education Network’s (C-BEN’s) Quality Framework for Postsecondary CBE Programs, 1 and the research team’s knowledge of the field to identify the design features of focus. AIR then convened an advisory group of CBE program administrators, staff, faculty, researchers, and other thought leaders, including C-BEN leaders. This advisory group provided preliminary testing and helped refine the survey items and response options. After incorporating the group’s feedback, AIR launched the survey in March 2023; it remained open through May 2023.
Because the number of institutions with CBE programs in which students are already enrolled is relatively limited (with many more in a planning phase), AIR opted to send a participation invitation to all institutions known to be offering at least one active CBE program, as well as many institutions last known to be in the planning phase in case they had launched a program for which they could respond. Institutions had the option of submitting one response on behalf of all their programs, particularly when they had a shared CBE model—or they could submit a different response for each program design. Of the 25 institutions, 23 chose the former. This is important for interpreting the data throughout this piece, as one response might represent dozens of programs following a single model, whereas at another institution, one response might represent one program. To analyze the data, AIR used a combination of descriptive analysis and thematic analysis of the responses to identify trends within and across domains. We emphasize exploring whether the field has converged around particular design choices, to help inform ongoing conversations. In summer 2023, the AIR Research team led five user-testing sessions with individual CBE researchers and practitioners. Feedback from these sessions was incorporated into the framework and potential use cases are highlighted in the “Looking Ahead” section of this brief. Methods 1 C-BEN’s Quality Framework offers quality standards for CBE programs, remaining agnostic about program model. This CBE Program Model Map tool is intended to complement that tool, offering a descriptive tool for program model design choices without any judgment about quality.
METHODS LOOKING AHEAD FLEXIBILITY IN CBE SUPPORT IN CBE COMPETENCY AND LEARNING 6 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF In total, representatives from 25 institutions—representing hundreds of CBE programs—responded to the CBE Program Model Map survey in spring 2023. While this number does not include every CBE program in operation at the time of this project (as of 2020, we estimated that number to be 128 institutions ), these data include information on nearly all the longest operating CBE programs, which we estimate represent more than 80% of the CBE learners enrolled nationally. The following graphics provide information on characteristics of institutions that participated in the survey.
As shown in Exhibit 1, survey participants represented four institution types, with approximately half representing community colleges. When assessing which institutions did not respond to the survey (to understand which institutions this may underrepresent), community colleges are disproportionately represented in that group. Institutional Characteristics Public 2-year INSTITUTION TYPE 48% Public 4-year 22% Private nonprofit 4-year 15% Private for-profit 4-year 15% EXHIBIT 1.

Generally, these respondents represent a mix of program types (including some that offer multiple types of programs/credentials). They also represent a mix of CBE locations within their institutional structure, although most are housed within a traditional school, college, or department, alongside comparable traditional programs. Program Location Within Institutional Structure Housed within a traditional college/ school or department within the institution alongside a comparable traditional degree Housed within a traditional college/school or department within the institution, no comparable traditional degree exists Housed in a workforce training/ non-credit division Separate online college within the institution Separate CBE college within the institution Institution only offers CBE programs 4% 19% 48% 19% 0% 11% METHODS LOOKING AHEAD FLEXIBILITY IN CBE SUPPORT IN CBE COMPETENCY AND LEARNING 8 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF In this research brief, we organize findings about whether the field has settled on specific design options centered around the learner’s perspective of design principles. This choice is distinct from the organization of the aforementioned CBE Program Model Map Framework, which is organized from an institutional perspective and generally aligns with the elements in C-BEN’s Quality Framework. That resource is primarily intended for use by institutions considering design or redesign of a program, including distinguishing choices that academic departments make themselves (such as assessment strategies) from choices that involve other units on campus (such as financial aid and registrars). This brief is intended to provide a snapshot of the field, and given the interest in how and whether CBE programs are meeting the value propositions for learners, the organization structure of the brief prioritizes a learner’s perspective in terms of the design principles most relevant to them.
Framing the Data METHODS LOOKING AHEAD FLEXIBILITY IN CBE SUPPORT IN CBE COMPETENCY AND LEARNING 9 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF The learner’s perspective of CBE program design elements differs from the institution’s view. The field has broadly identified three commonly cited value propositions of CBE, which focus on the intended outcomes for learners. These include the reasons learners might choose, or fare better in, a CBE program: expanding access and promoting success; reducing price/cost to learners; and ensuring quality in learning outcomes. These value propositions interact with and support one another. For example, reducing the cost to learners likely contributes to improving access and success, too. Therefore, we represent them in overlapping shapes. Analyzing CBE Program Model Designs A Learner-Centered Perspective EXHIBIT 4. VALUE PROPOSITIONS FOR LEARNERS IN CBE ACCESS AND SUCCESS COST (PRICE) LEARNING OUTCOMES METHODS LOOKING AHEAD FLEXIBILITY IN CBE SUPPORT IN CBE COMPETENCY AND LEARNING 10 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF When we unpack the logic of how CBE programs might fulfill these value propositions, they generally map to three CBE Program Model Design Principles (design principles): • designing the curriculum and assessments around competency and learning (competencies and demonstration of competencies); • allowing learner flexibility (in pathways, timing or pacing, and ways of demonstrating competencies); and • providing support for learners (emphasizing personalized support, including both academic and nonacademic). The theory of change for design principles is complex; just as the value propositions are not mutually exclusive, the mapping between design principles and value propositions is not one to one. Each of these affect learners’ experiences, which then in turn shapes the potential value propositions. As demonstrated in Exhibit 5, no single design feature fully addresses any one of these principles, and each design principle might contribute to multiple value propositions. Exhibit 5 demonstrates the primary relationships between design principles and value propositions that are clear and apparent in current CBE programs. For example, the connection between flexibility and cost has been a primary emphasis in some CBE programs—if a program allows learners to accelerate and charges them a subscription fee per time period, the learners can experience a lower total cost of completing their degree. Similarly, flexibility can contribute to expanded and more equitable access and success by enabling more learners to make postsecondary education “work with their lives” because fewer conflicts with jobs, family, or other issues emerge. In terms of support, the expanded and personalized support—such as coaching—provided in many CBE models might facilitate expanded and equitable access and success for the same reasons that wraparound supports do in traditional programs. Finally, the competency and learning orientation of programs—emphasizing each learner demonstrating each competency, distinct from relative grading and sorting in traditional programs—might improve learning outcomes and in more equitable ways. EXHIBIT 5. RELATIONSHIPS BETWEEN CBE PROGRAM DESIGN PRINCIPLES AND CBE VALUE PROPOSITIONS ACCESS AND SUCCESS COST (PRICE) LEARNING OUTCOMES FLEXIBILITY SUPPORT COMPETENCY AND LEARNING DESIGN PRINCIPLES VALUE PROPOSITIONS FOR LEARNERS EXPERIENCES OF LEARNERS METHODS LOOKING AHEAD FLEXIBILITY IN CBE SUPPORT IN CBE COMPETENCY AND LEARNING 11 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF Other connections not currently represented in Exhibit 5 are plausible and even likely, but less well understood or present in current programs. For example, we have not drawn arrows between flexibility and learning outcomes. In that example, if programs make learning and mastery more transparent to learners along the way via design centered on learning and demonstration of mastery, that may provide additional motivation and clarity that helps learners focus and persist toward degree completion. This relationship, however, is not yet well understood in the context of CBE programs; as the field and research develops, we endorse updating this graphic to represent these connections. CBE programs may be designed for different populations with different priorities among the value propositions; therefore, we expected to find variation among existing CBE program designs. No single design would work well for all learners, all fields, or all institutional settings. In the future, researchers can use this framework to explore variation in design choices based on the nuanced interests and needs of the populations the CBE program intends to serve. For instance, if an institution building a CBE program has a local industry that will support its current employees’ enrolling with tuition reimbursement, as long as the program fits around their work schedule (imagining that their work schedule varies by week, as so many schedules do), that institution may opt to emphasize the flexibility and support design features but may be less focused on the options that reduce cost—since employers will cover it. As an alternative, a program that serves many low-income learners, or perhaps seasonal workers, may emphasize the flexibility design features that maximize the possibility for learners to accelerate; if they have a gap between jobs during which they can enroll full time, perhaps the benefit of CBE is enabling success, or degree attainment, at an accelerated pace and lower cost. These examples are not exhaustive but intend to provide inspiration for future analyses and exploration that use this resource. Our intent is for this resource to support exploration about the design features that help fulfill the value propositions of CBE for the variety of learners—each with different interests, strengths, and constraints—in support of a more equitable postsecondary education system for all.
METHODS LOOKING AHEAD FLEXIBILITY IN CBE SUPPORT IN CBE COMPETENCY AND LEARNING 12 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF For the purposes of analysis and interpretation, we organized CBE program design features by each of the three design principles: flexibility, support, and competency and learning. We chose to align each design feature with only one design principle based on the strongest theory of change; however, as we described in the previous section, there may be theoretical connections between an individual design feature and multiple principles. We present each feature independently, although it is likely that some design choices “hang together” in practice. However, our analysis did not show clear types, so we have opted to avoid exploring connections or bundles of choices. The following sections are broken out by design principle. Bar graphs show the design features and design options associated with a given principle. The length of each bar and associated percentage indicates the frequency at which a design option was selected by respondents. Although representatives from 27 distinct programs responded to the survey, some respondents chose to skip over individual items (e.g., because an item did not apply to their program), and therefore the n varies from item to item. Data in This Brief Most items required respondents to select one option (generally the option that most closely aligned with their program design, even if it did not perfectly describe their model). As a result, most graphs will add to 100%. However, some items were optional (i.e., select an option if applicable to your program), resulting in totals less than 100%. In other cases—which we have labeled “Exploratory Items” in offset sections in this brief—the survey asked respondents to “select all that applied.” These items were often follow-up questions included for the purpose of narrowing down response options in future versions of the framework. These questions are presented separately because the interpretations of them differ. For example, because respondents could select multiple options, the total percentage for each design feature may not add to 100. Others were simply a starting point; for example, the exploratory item related to disaggregating data was presented as a “Yes” or “No” question for the purpose of identifying a need for additional questions related to data use for continuous improvement.
While the data presented in this brief represents a snapshot in time, the accompanying framework is designed to be a “living document” that can be updated as the CBE field evolves. As such, the framework was updated in summer 2023 on the basis of feedback from a user-testing group made up of researchers and practitioners. Items that have been updated in the framework are denoted in this brief with an asterisk*. METHODS LOOKING AHEAD FLEXIBILITY IN CBE SUPPORT IN CBE COMPETENCY AND LEARNING 13 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF Flexibility in CBE Flexibility is a key component of any learner-centered CBE program design, as flexibility allows learners agency while also allowing learners to “fit education into their lives”—an important equity priority and a common interest for adult learners, in particular, who may have family commitments, jobs, and/or health concerns. This flexibility stands in contrast to instructor-centered models of traditional higher education, which focus on class schedules based on faculty scheduling and standard term lengths. The degree and types of flexibility that CBE programs offer, though, may vary. This section captures program design choices that affect the amount of autonomy learners have in shaping their educational experiences, including aspects such as the way and the times learners can enroll and move on (pace/progress), and whether they have control of their journey through the curriculum and opportunities to personalize their paths, as well as pricing and financial aid decisions that either facilitate or limit certain kinds of flexibility. Each feature is presented separately, although in practice, some design choices may be dependent on others.
Flexibility We observe moderate levels of variation for design elements pertaining to time flexibility for learners. In contrast, financial and content flexibility for learners is more consistent with what we typically observe from traditional programs. This trend may reflect the nature of working within existing structures, or it may reflect careful experimentation with degrees of flexibility before an institution considers moving toward more flexible options. We found only two areas (not including the exploratory item) where more than 75 percent of institutions had made the same program decisions, both of which were elements commonly observed in traditional education, and neither of which relate to flexibility in terms of timing. This not only speaks to amount of variation pertaining to flexibility but suggests that the field has not yet coalesced around a particular set of design choices in the area and that an understanding regarding best practices pertaining to flexibility in CBE is still emerging.

7% 53% 40% METHODS FLEXIBILITY IN CBE SUPPORT IN CBE COMPETENCY AND LEARNING LOOKING AHEAD 17 POSTSECONDARY COMPETENCY-BASED EDUCATION PROGRAM MODEL MAP RESEARCH BRIEF Support in CBE Supports are a growing topic across higher education, but CBE programs in particular often incorporate or rethink support structures, services, and programs, particularly those tailored to adult learners. These supports can be an important equity priority, particularly in the context of granting learners additional flexibility and enabling personalization of their journey, including the way they engage with people—and with whom—along the way. CBE programs are restructuring and reimagining these structures, which we capture here. The support design category captures design element choices regarding structural and programmatic efforts and resources provided to support the CBE learner, including at entry and throughout the learning journey. Support Programs demonstrate a wide variety of design choices, with only three areas registering more than 75 percent of respondents, and these choices represent programs choosing to stay consistent with institution-wide practices. This could indicate many things, a lack of available resources to establish unique and/or exclusive services for CBE students, the number of current students not warranting the creation of unique processes and services, or a determination that the resources already made available by the institutions are sufficient. enriched_points.expanded_content: Competency-Based Education (CBE) is gaining traction, with over 50% of U.S. schools adopting its principles, driven by a need for personalized, mastery-focused learning.
CBE models are flexible and adaptable, making them suitable for diverse educational settings and scalable across institutions, allowing for widespread adoption and integration.
CBE promotes continuous skill development, aligning with workforce demands and encouraging learners to pursue ongoing education, ensuring relevance in a rapidly changing job market.
